Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time each week, but with 11 online courses running simultaneously, I was constantly putting out fires. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often spend hours working through problems physically or building things to understand concepts, which took way more time than just reading. I'd cram in study sessions whenever I could find gaps between my extracurriculars and everything else going on.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how stressed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely touch certain courses, then suddenly realize I had multiple deadlines converging and go into panic mode. I tried to be consistent, but honestly, consistency just wasn't realistic with everything on my plate. Deadlines basically dictated my entire schedule.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline or realizing I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a new assignment or announcement and that would prompt me to log in. I rarely logged in just to browse or stay on top of things proactively—it was almost always reactive. The anxiety of falling behind was my main motivator.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my routine—it was more like this thing I had to force myself to deal with. I'd go through phases where I'd try to check it daily, but that never stuck. With so many courses, the LMS just felt overwhelming rather than helpful, so I avoided it more than I should have.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful when I had time to watch them—I watched a lot of video content because seeing things demonstrated helped me understand better than just reading. Discussion posts occasionally gave me different perspectives on tricky concepts. But honestly, with my learning style, I got more out of actually doing things hands-on than any LMS resource could provide.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources. There was just too much content across all my courses, and I had to prioritize ruthlessly. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it usually got pushed to the bottom of my list and stayed there. I also barely engaged with peer interactions—I just didn't have the bandwidth.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be assessed on or if it involved actually doing something rather than passive consumption. I'm kinesthetic—I need to work through problems, build things, apply concepts. Anything that let me get my hands dirty, metaphorically speaking, felt valuable. Reading walls of text felt like a waste when I could be practicing.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist for me this semester. With 11 courses and everything else going on, I had to triage constantly. Required activities got done—sometimes barely—but optional ones? I couldn't justify the time. It's not that I didn't see their value; I just physically couldn't fit them in without sacrificing sleep or my sanity.
Q9: Absolutely. A huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download materials once and then work through them on paper, building diagrams, doing practice problems by hand, sometimes even creating physical models to understand concepts. I'd use external YouTube videos, textbooks from the library, and study with friends in person. The LMS was just the starting point.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened away from the computer. I'd grab the key materials and then spend hours at my desk or in the library working through problems manually. My exam scores reflect that I understood the material deeply, but the LMS wouldn't show those late-night sessions where I was physically writing out solutions and testing my understanding through practice.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture slides or problem sets once, then never look at them on the LMS again. I'd print things out and annotate them heavily, or save videos to watch offline when I had time. My actual engagement with the content was much deeper than my login frequency would suggest—I just preferred working with physical materials.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't bring myself to engage. When deadlines piled up across multiple courses, I'd feel paralyzed. My attendance dropped because I was so overwhelmed trying to keep up with everything else. The CBT sessions I had helped me work through some of that, but the overwhelm was real and frequent.
Q13: Massively. The sheer workload of 11 courses meant I was constantly exhausted and making impossible choices about what to prioritize. Confusion about requirements in some courses made me avoid those LMS pages entirely. My extracurriculars added another layer of time pressure. I'd often access the LMS in a fog of fatigue, barely processing what I was looking at.
Q14: Yeah, definitely. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials at 2 AM, technically logged in and clicking around, but my brain was completely checked out. I'd watch videos while half-asleep and retain nothing. There were times I'd open readings and just stare at them, too anxious to actually focus. The LMS tracked my presence but not my mental state.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made it hard to start things—I'd feel overwhelmed looking at the requirements and just... freeze. My assignment completion rate was terrible even though I understood the material for exams. It's like my brain couldn't handle the sustained effort of assignments while managing everything else.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage with the LMS. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due. My whole relationship with the platform was deadline-driven—I'd ignore courses for weeks then frantically catch up. It wasn't healthy, but it was survival mode.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. When I got good feedback on exams, it motivated me to keep pushing through. But feedback on assignments I'd rushed felt discouraging—I knew I could do better but didn't have the time. Sometimes feedback came too late to help me improve before the next deadline. The positive exam results kept me going despite everything.
Q18: The course structure honestly made things harder for me. Having so much content spread across the LMS with no clear priority markers meant I was constantly guessing what mattered most. I would have benefited from more hands-on activities built into the structure. The flexibility that works for some students just gave me more rope to hang myself with.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement harder. When I'd log in and see dozens of resources, my anxiety would spike and I'd sometimes just log out. Courses that had clearer pathways and fewer but more focused materials were easier to engage with. The discussion requirements felt forced rather than organic, which made me avoid them.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat—I had decent login activity and watched a lot of videos, but my assignment completion was poor. For me, it was anxiety and overwhelm. I was trying to learn, genuinely engaging with content, but the step of actually producing and submitting work felt insurmountable sometimes. The pressure of being evaluated paralyzed me.
Q21: That also partially describes me. My exam scores were excellent despite low overall LMS engagement because I was learning effectively offline. I'd master material through hands-on practice and external resources, then perform well on exams. The LMS activity doesn't capture someone studying from printed notes at 3 AM or working through problems on paper.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, time spent. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and offline learning. They can't tell if someone is staring blankly at a screen or deeply focused. They miss the anxiety behind erratic patterns, the learning happening away from the computer, the real reasons behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely grappling with the material and trying to understand it deeply, not just going through the motions. For me, it's about that hands-on connection with concepts—building understanding through practice and application. It's not about how many times I logged in; it's about whether the knowledge actually stuck and whether I can use it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my constant anxiety, the hours I spent studying offline, or how hard I was actually trying despite my poor assignment completion. It wouldn't capture the late nights working through problems by hand or the genuine understanding I developed. It also wouldn't show how overwhelmed I felt managing 11 courses while trying to maintain some semblance of balance in my life.
